danger_signals.visual_form: Animated hostile NPCs with distinct character designs
Environmental hazards integrated into boss arenas (rotating rooms with spike floors, raised/lowered platforms, steel cage surrounds)
danger_signals.meaning: Escalated danger and complexity; boss-level threat

danger_signals.visual_form: Multiple paths, dead ends, complex navigation in Stages 3 and 9
danger_signals.meaning: Disorientation and extended exposure to enemy encounters

danger_signals.visual_form: Pickupable sticks, guns, grenades scattered in levels
danger_signals.meaning: Escalated combat intensity ahead; environment contains lethal threats

safety_signals.meaning: Opportunity to recover health and sustain combat capability

safety_signals.visual_form: AI-controlled second fighter on screen (randomly assigned per Japanese mode)
safety_signals.meaning: Cooperative support reduces individual player danger

safety_signals.visual_form: Visual/behavioral state change giving injured fighters energy burst
safety_signals.meaning: Temporary invulnerability or damage boost; reversal of danger state

faction_indicators.color_identity: Primary character palette varies by fighter (Slash wears yellow boots; color changed in Western version for red character)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Character archetypes: street brawler, police officer, judo master, ninja, professional wrestler
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Western version replaces Japanese character names (Norton→Hack, Bild→Slash) suggesting identity shift or regionalization rather than factional opposition
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Boss stages assigned to each character suggest territorial/professional domains (police officer: construction platform; ninja: dojo; wrestler: wrestling ring)

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Named enemy types with mechanical/dehumanized identities (Japanese: K-M308, R.E-Gun, Hig-E103, J-Worker, N-J, Iron Leg, Begaro, Slider, Iceman; Western: Zero, Heckler, Flynn, Led Hed, Phantom, Fnord, Butch, Cyberhed, Dieter)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Final boss Dieter/Iceman has extendable/flexible staff, suggesting unique mechanical threat distinct from generic enemy types
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Enemies spawned across multiple stage environments (streets, temples, dojos, wrestling rings, construction sites); no unified faction visual identity documented

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Martial artist with extendable staff; unused Vs. Mode icon suggests special status
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Distinct from generic enemy roster; playable via cheat code but maintains visual separation in standard gameplay
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Final stage arena (stage 4) represents culmination of protagonist journey; mechanical/martial mastery vs. street-level combat

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Linear stage layouts (Japanese version) with straightforward progression paths
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Fewer enemies, basic combat patterns, health pickups abundant

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Maze-like environments with dead ends (Western version Stages 3 and 9); arena-specific hazards (rotating spike floors, raising platforms)
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Enemy density increases; boss-tier opponents with unique move sets and environmental interactions

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Dedicated arena stages with integrated environmental hazards; final boss uses extendable staff weapon
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Single opponent with advanced AI, pattern-based attacks, arena-manipulation abilities

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Visual damage state on player character; proximity to defeat
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Angry Mode triggers, providing temporary invulnerability/damage boost—visual manifestation of desperation mechanic

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Character select screen background reflects game logo; character skin color altered (red character); kanji removed from map screens in Western version
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Groin-kick animation removed in Western version (censorship); maze stages added in Western version (gameplay expansion); different character names establish narrative identity

environmental_storytelling.example: Police officer Slash/Bild fights in construction platform (authority/infrastructure); Ninja Kazan fights in dojo with rotating spike traps (training/discipline); Wrestler Wendy Milan fights in wrestling ring with rope interactions (athletic spectacle)

Multiple stage environments (streets, temples, dojos, construction sites, rings) suggest varied societal/industrial locations; enemy naming (K-M308, R.E-Gun, Led Hed) implies technological/mechanical threat

environmental_storytelling.technique: Weapon placement signals environmental danger level
environmental_storytelling.example: Sticks, guns, grenades scattered throughout levels indicate escalating combat intensity; weapons tied to stage hazards (grenades near industrial sites)

environmental_storytelling.technique: Maze vs. linear layout difference communicates pacing
environmental_storytelling.example: Japanese version's linear progression allows straightforward narrative; Western version's maze stages in 3 and 9 create exploration/confusion, extending engagement and danger

environmental_storytelling.technique: Boss stage customization indicates character domain
environmental_storytelling.example: Each playable character has unique boss arena matching profession/theme (cage for street brawler, construction for police, temple for judo master), visually narrativizing their expertise and social position

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Active stance, responsive to input, full movement range and attack options
Visual damage representation; reduced effectiveness until health restored
Temporary invulnerability or damage boost; visual indicator of state change
Spawned at stage-appropriate locations; waits for player approach
Attacks with stage-appropriate patterns; responds to player position and attacks
Advanced AI patterns; arena-interaction mechanics (Slash's boss manipulates platform height; Kazan's dojo rotates with spike integration); unique weapons/tools (Dieter's extendable staff)
Fights alongside player; reduces individual threat burden; indicates faction solidarity
Distinct from generic enemy pool; extendable staff suggests unique martial mastery; playable state via cheat suggests narrative importance and mechanical complexity

notes: Brawl Brothers uses minimal explicit faction visual communication; protagonist/antagonist distinction relies on positioning and enemy naming conventions rather than color or symbol systems. Environmental arena theming (dojo, wrestling ring, temple, construction) serves as primary world-state and character-archetype communication. Regional differences (Japanese vs. Western) fundamentally alter player experience: linear vs. maze layouts change danger perception; censored animations affect character identity; character name changes (Norton→Hack, Bild→Slash) suggest localization rather than narrative factionalism. The game's danger/safety communication is primarily mechanical (health pickups, Angry Mode, environmental hazards) rather than visual-narrative. Boss encounter design is the strongest manifestation of world-state escalation, with each character's arena reflecting professional/thematic identity and increasing mechanical complexity.
